questions.text: What software company is headquartered in Redmond, Washington?
questions.explanation: Microsoft was founded by Bill Gates and Paul Allen in 1975, with headquarters in Redmond, Washington.

questions.text: What is a word, phrase, or number that reads the same backward as forward?
questions.explanation: A palindrome reads the same forwards and backwards, like 'radar' or 'level'.

questions.text: What is the world's largest retailer as of 2026?
questions.explanation: Walmart remains the world's largest retailer by revenue, with over 10,500 stores worldwide.

questions.text: Which day of the week does the Jewish Sabbath begin?
questions.explanation: The Jewish Sabbath (Shabbat) begins at sunset on Friday and ends at nightfall on Saturday.

questions.text: What phone company produced the 3310?
questions.explanation: The Nokia 3310 was released in 2000 and became famous for its durability and long battery life.

questions.text: What company was initially known as 'Blue Ribbon Sports'?
questions.explanation: Blue Ribbon Sports was founded in 1964 by Bill Bowerman and Phil Knight, becoming Nike in 1971.

questions.text: How many elements are in the periodic table?
questions.explanation: There are 118 confirmed elements in the periodic table, from Hydrogen (1) to Oganesson (118).

questions.text: Which planet is closest to the sun?
questions.explanation: Mercury is the closest planet to the Sun, orbiting at an average distance of 58 million kilometers.

questions.text: How many bones do we have in an ear?
questions.explanation: The middle ear contains three tiny bones: the hammer (malleus), anvil (incus), and stirrup (stapes).

questions.text: What is the chemical element with the symbol Fe?
questions.explanation: Fe comes from the Latin word 'ferrum', meaning iron.

questions.text: What is the smallest unit of matter?
questions.explanation: The atom is the smallest unit of matter that retains the properties of an element.

questions.text: What is the process by which a liquid changes into a gas?
questions.explanation: Evaporation occurs when liquid molecules gain enough energy to become gas.

questions.text: What was the name of the crime boss who was head of the feared Chicago Outfit?
questions.explanation: Al Capone ran the Chicago Outfit during the Prohibition era in the 1920s.

questions.text: What Renaissance artist is buried in Rome's Pantheon?
questions.explanation: Raphael, the famous Renaissance painter, is buried in the Pantheon in Rome.

questions.text: In what year did World War II end?
questions.explanation: WWII ended in 1945 with Germany's surrender in May and Japan's surrender in September.

questions.text: Which famous document begins with 'We hold these truths to be self-evident'?
questions.explanation: The Declaration of Independence begins with these famous words, adopted on July 4, 1776.

questions.text: Who was the first woman to win a Nobel Prize?
questions.explanation: Marie Curie won the Nobel Prize in Physics in 1903, sharing it with her husband Pierre.

questions.text: What was the code name for the Allied invasion of Normandy?
questions.explanation: Operation Overlord was the codename for the D-Day invasion on June 6, 1944.

questions.text: Which country has won the most World Cups?
questions.explanation: Brazil has won 5 World Cups (1958, 1962, 1970, 1994, 2002).

questions.text: What does FIFA stand for in English?
questions.explanation: FIFA stands for Fédération Internationale de Football Association, the governing body of world football.

questions.text: Simone Biles is famous for her skill in what sport?
questions.explanation: Simone Biles is considered one of the greatest gymnasts of all time with 32 Olympic and World Championship medals.

questions.text: What was the first name of Argentinian soccer star Maradona?
questions.explanation: Diego Maradona is considered one of the greatest footballers in history.

questions.text: What is the national sport of Japan?
questions.explanation: Sumo wrestling is Japan's national sport, with a history spanning over 1,500 years.

questions.text: What is a group of crows called?
questions.explanation: A group of crows is called a 'murder' of crows, a term from medieval folklore.

questions.text: What is the only mammal capable of true flight?
questions.explanation: Bats are the only mammals that can truly fly, using their wing membranes stretched between elongated fingers.

questions.text: What is the term for the study of birds?
questions.explanation: Ornithology comes from Greek 'ornis' meaning bird and 'logos' meaning study.

questions.text: Where is the Louvre Museum located?
questions.explanation: The Louvre Museum is in Paris, France, and is the world's largest art museum.

questions.text: Who is the singer known for the hit 'Bad Guy'?
questions.explanation: Billie Eilish released 'Bad Guy' in 2019, which became her first number-one single.

questions.text: What art movement is Pablo Picasso associated with?
questions.explanation: Picasso co-founded Cubism, which revolutionized European painting and sculpture.

questions.text: What musical instrument has 88 keys?
questions.explanation: A standard piano has 88 keys: 52 white keys and 36 black keys.

questions.text: Which country has the highest life expectancy as of 2026?
questions.explanation: Monaco has the highest life expectancy at around 89 years due to excellent healthcare and lifestyle.

questions.text: What is the most common surname in the United States?
questions.explanation: Smith is the most common surname in the U.S., with over 2.4 million people bearing the name.

questions.text: What art form is described as 'decorative handwriting or handwritten lettering'?
questions.explanation: Calligraphy is the art of beautiful handwriting, often used for formal documents and art.

questions.text: What is acrophobia a fear of?
questions.explanation: Acrophobia is an extreme fear of heights, from Greek 'acron' (peak) and 'phobos' (fear).

questions.text: What is the name of the Chinese philosophical system that emphasizes harmony with nature?
questions.explanation: Taoism, founded by Lao Tzu, emphasizes living in harmony with the Tao (natural order).

questions.text: What is the 4th letter of the Greek alphabet?
questions.explanation: Delta is the fourth letter of the Greek alphabet, represented as Δ (capital) or δ (lowercase).

questions.text: How many dots appear on a pair of dice?
questions.explanation: Each die has 21 dots (1+2+3+4+5+6 = 21), so two dice total 42 dots.

questions.text: Which planet has the most moons?
questions.explanation: Saturn has 146 confirmed moons, the most of any planet, including Titan and Enceladus.

questions.text: Where is the strongest human muscle located?
questions.explanation: The masseter muscle in the jaw can apply up to 200 pounds of force on the molars.

questions.text: Which is the only body part that is fully grown from birth?
questions.explanation: Eyes are nearly full size at birth, only growing slightly during childhood to reach adult size.

questions.text: What is the outermost layer of the Earth's atmosphere called?
questions.explanation: The exosphere is the outermost atmospheric layer, extending from 700 km to 10,000 km above Earth.

questions.text: What is the process by which plants convert sunlight to energy?
questions.explanation: Photosynthesis converts light energy into chemical energy stored in glucose.

questions.text: What scientific theory proposed that Earth revolves around the sun?
questions.explanation: Heliocentrism, proposed by Copernicus, placed the sun at the center of the solar system.

questions.text: What year was the United Nations established?
questions.explanation: The UN was established on October 24, 1945, after WWII, with 51 founding members.

questions.text: Who was the last Tsar of Russia?
questions.explanation: Nicholas II ruled from 1894 until his abdication in 1917 during the Russian Revolution.

questions.text: Roald Amundsen was the first man to reach the South Pole, but where was he from?
questions.explanation: Roald Amundsen was Norwegian, reaching the South Pole on December 14, 1911.

questions.text: Who famously crossed the Alps with elephants on the way to war with the Romans?
questions.explanation: Hannibal crossed the Alps in 218 BCE with 37 war elephants during the Second Punic War.

questions.text: The Parthenon Marbles are controversially located in what museum?
questions.explanation: The Parthenon Marbles have been in the British Museum since being removed by Lord Elgin in 1801.

questions.text: What event marked the end of the Roman Empire in the West?
questions.explanation: The fall of Rome in 476 CE marked the end of the Western Roman Empire.

questions.text: Which ancient Chinese dynasty is often considered the 'golden age' of Chinese civilization?
questions.explanation: The Han Dynasty (206 BCE-220 CE) is considered China's golden age of cultural and technological advancement.

questions.text: What Renaissance artist painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling?
questions.explanation: Michelangelo painted the Sistine Chapel ceiling between 1508 and 1512.

questions.text: What was the name of the German military strategy of rapid, overwhelming attacks?
questions.explanation: Blitzkrieg (lightning war) used fast, coordinated attacks to overwhelm defenses.

questions.text: What was the primary cause of the Great Irish Famine?
questions.explanation: Phytophthora infestans (potato blight) destroyed potato crops, leading to the famine from 1845-1852.

questions.text: What was the name of the peace treaty that ended World War I?
questions.explanation: The Treaty of Versailles was signed on June 28, 1919, formally ending WWI.

questions.text: In what city is the NFL Hall of Fame located?
questions.explanation: The Pro Football Hall of Fame is in Canton, Ohio, where the NFL was founded in 1920.

questions.text: Where does the Tour de France finish each year?
questions.explanation: The Tour de France traditionally finishes on the Avenue des Champs-Élysées in Paris.

questions.text: What do you call it when a bowler makes three strikes in a row?
questions.explanation: Three consecutive strikes in bowling is called a 'turkey', dating from tournament prizes in the 1800s.

questions.text: What height is a regulation NBA basket?
questions.explanation: NBA rims are exactly 10 feet (3.05 meters) from the playing floor.

questions.text: What sporting event has a strict dress code of all white?
questions.explanation: Wimbledon requires players to wear almost entirely white, a tradition since 1877.

questions.text: What is the world's fastest bird?
questions.explanation: The Peregrine falcon reaches speeds over 240 mph (386 km/h) during its hunting dive (stoop).

questions.text: Who was the Ancient Greek God of the Sun?
questions.explanation: Apollo was the god of the sun, music, poetry, and prophecy in Greek mythology.

questions.text: Aureolin is a shade of what color?
questions.explanation: Aureolin is a vivid yellow pigment, also known as cobalt yellow.

questions.text: How many faces does a Dodecahedron have?
questions.explanation: A dodecahedron has 12 regular pentagonal faces, 20 vertices, and 30 edges.

questions.text: How many minutes are in a full week?
questions.explanation: 7 days × 24 hours × 60 minutes = 10,080 minutes in a week.

questions.text: December 26 is known by what name in Ireland?
questions.explanation: In Ireland, December 26 is St. Stephen's Day, named after the first Christian martyr.

questions.text: What is the scientific theory that explains the origin of the universe?
questions.explanation: The Big Bang Theory explains the universe's origin from a singularity about 13.8 billion years ago.

questions.text: What is the name of the world's first artificial satellite?
questions.explanation: Sputnik 1 was launched by the Soviet Union on October 4, 1957, starting the Space Age.

questions.text: Who discovered that the Earth revolves around the sun?
questions.explanation: Copernicus proposed the heliocentric model in the 16th century, though Aristarchus suggested it earlier.

questions.text: Which planet in the Milky Way is the hottest?
questions.explanation: Venus is the hottest planet with surface temperatures up to 471°C (880°F) due to its thick atmosphere.

questions.text: How many hearts does an octopus have?
questions.explanation: Octopuses have three hearts: two pump blood to the gills, one pumps to the rest of the body.

questions.text: What is a group of pandas known as?
questions.explanation: A group of pandas is called an 'embarrassment' due to their awkward, clumsy movements.

questions.text: What animal has the longest tongue?
questions.explanation: Giraffes have 18-20 inch (45-50 cm) tongues, the longest of any land animal.

questions.text: What is the loudest animal on Earth?
questions.explanation: Sperm whale clicks reach 230 decibels, the loudest sound produced by any animal.

questions.text: What animal has the largest brain relative to body size?
questions.explanation: Dolphins have the highest brain-to-body ratio after humans, with complex intelligence.

questions.text: What animal had the most powerful bite in the world?
questions.explanation: The Nile crocodile has a bite force of up to 5,000 PSI, the strongest measured.

questions.text: What animal has the longest lifespan?
questions.explanation: Glass sponges can live for over 10,000 years - the longest of any animal.

questions.text: Which powerful Mongol ruler conquered a vast empire stretching from Asia to Europe?
questions.explanation: Genghis Khan founded the Mongol Empire, the largest contiguous land empire in history.

questions.text: In what decade was the internet created?
questions.explanation: The internet's precursor, ARPANET, was developed in the 1960s by the U.S. Department of Defense.

questions.text: What was the name of the first computer virus?
questions.explanation: Creeper was created in 1971 as an experiment, displaying 'I'm the creeper, catch me if you can!'

questions.text: Who was the world's highest-paid athlete in 2023?
questions.explanation: Cristiano Ronaldo earned $260 million in 2023, highest among all athletes.

questions.text: Who wrote The Catcher in the Rye?
questions.explanation: J.D. Salinger published 'The Catcher in the Rye' in 1951, a classic of modern American literature.

questions.text: Which composer became deaf later in life?
questions.explanation: Beethoven began losing his hearing in his late 20s and was completely deaf by his mid-40s.

questions.text: What is the hardest natural substance on Earth?
questions.explanation: Diamond is the hardest natural mineral, rated 10 on the Mohs hardness scale.

questions.text: What is the chemical symbol for gold?
questions.explanation: Au comes from Latin 'aurum', meaning gold.

questions.text: What is a group of lions called?
questions.explanation: A group of lions is called a pride, typically consisting of related females and their offspring.

questions.text: Who wrote 1984?
questions.explanation: George Orwell published 'Nineteen Eighty-Four' in 1949, depicting totalitarian surveillance.

questions.text: What gas do plants absorb from the atmosphere?
questions.explanation: Plants absorb CO2 for photosynthesis, releasing oxygen as a byproduct.

questions.text: What is the capital of Iceland?
questions.explanation: Reykjavik is Iceland's capital and northernmost capital of a sovereign state.

questions.text: Who directed Jurassic Park?
questions.explanation: Steven Spielberg directed Jurassic Park (1993), revolutionizing CGI effects.

questions.text: What ancient civilization built Machu Picchu?
questions.explanation: The Inca civilization built Machu Picchu around 1450 CE as an estate for Emperor Pachacuti.

questions.text: Who was known as the 'Maid of Orléans'?
questions.explanation: Joan of Arc led French forces during the Hundred Years' War and was canonized as a saint.

questions.text: Mount Kilimanjaro is located in which country?
questions.explanation: Mount Kilimanjaro is in Tanzania, near the Kenyan border, and is Africa's highest mountain.

questions.text: What is the largest mammal on Earth?
questions.explanation: The blue whale is the largest animal ever known to exist, reaching up to 100 feet and 200 tons.

questions.text: In The Matrix, does Neo take the blue pill or the red pill?
questions.explanation: Neo takes the red pill to learn the truth about the Matrix.

questions.text: Which artist is famous for painting water lilies?
questions.explanation: Monet painted approximately 250 water lily paintings at his garden in Giverny.

questions.text: What is the tallest building in the world as of 2025?
questions.explanation: Burj Khalifa in Dubai is 828 meters (2,717 ft) tall, the world's tallest building.

questions.text: Who sculpted David?
questions.explanation: Michelangelo carved David from 1501-1504, a masterpiece of Renaissance sculpture.

questions.text: Which spice is derived from dried crocus flowers?
questions.explanation: Saffron comes from Crocus sativus flowers, requiring 75,000 flowers for 1 pound of spice.

questions.text: Which Shakespeare play features the character Iago?
questions.explanation: Iago is the villainous antagonist in Shakespeare's tragedy 'Othello'.

questions.text: How many legs does a lobster have?
questions.explanation: Lobsters have 10 legs, including four pairs of walking legs and one pair of claws.

questions.text: Which planet is known as the red planet?
questions.explanation: Mars appears red due to iron oxide (rust) on its surface.

questions.text: What is the capital city of Canada?
questions.explanation: Ottawa is Canada's capital, chosen by Queen Victoria in 1857.

questions.text: Who discovered penicillin?
questions.explanation: Alexander Fleming discovered penicillin in 1928, revolutionizing medicine with antibiotics.

questions.text: Who sings 'Pocketful of Sunshine'?
questions.explanation: Natasha Bedingfield released 'Pocketful of Sunshine' in 2007 as the title track of her album.

questions.text: What is the main ingredient in baba ghanoush?
questions.explanation: Baba ghanoush is made from mashed roasted eggplant, tahini, olive oil, and lemon juice.

questions.text: Which country hosted the 2021 Summer Olympics?
questions.explanation: The 2020 Olympics were postponed to 2021 due to COVID-19, still held in Tokyo, Japan.

questions.text: What is the world's smallest continent?
questions.explanation: Australia is the smallest continent (including Oceania), covering 8.5 million square kilometers.

questions.text: Who is Colin Jost's famous wife?
questions.explanation: Colin Jost married Scarlett Johansson in October 2020 in a private ceremony.

questions.text: Who voiced Sulley in Monsters, Inc.?
questions.explanation: John Goodman voiced James P. 'Sulley' Sullivan in Monsters, Inc. (2001).

questions.text: In Toy Story, what is Woody's last name?
questions.explanation: Woody's full name is Woody Pride, sheriff of Woody's Roundup.

questions.text: Who wrote Pride and Prejudice?
questions.explanation: Jane Austen published Pride and Prejudice in 1813, her most famous novel.

questions.text: Which city is known as The Big Apple?
questions.explanation: New York City was nicknamed 'The Big Apple' in jazz musicians' slang in the 1920s.

questions.text: Which city is known as The City of Lights?
questions.explanation: Paris was one of the first cities to have street lighting, earning this nickname.

questions.text: Which city is known as The Emerald City?
questions.explanation: Seattle is called the Emerald City for its lush evergreen forests.

questions.text: Who was the first person on the moon?
questions.explanation: Neil Armstrong stepped onto the moon on July 20, 1969, saying 'One small step for man...'

questions.text: What is the human body's largest organ?
questions.explanation: Skin is the largest organ, covering about 20 square feet and comprising 15% of body weight.

questions.text: What year did the first iPhone release?
questions.explanation: The first iPhone was announced by Steve Jobs on January 9, 2007, released later that year.

questions.text: Which ocean lies between Africa and Australia?
questions.explanation: The Indian Ocean separates Africa and Australia, covering about 70 million square kilometers.

questions.text: Who won the 2023 Women's World Cup?
questions.explanation: Spain defeated England 1-0 to win their first Women's World Cup in 2023.

questions.text: What is the national flower of Japan?
questions.explanation: Cherry blossom (sakura) is Japan's national flower, symbolizing renewal and transience.
